author: @off6848
text:
Bro it's not a friction turbine. Tesla explicitly mentions this in his patent. Its actually an electric turbine why because its not supposed to move the discs with friction quite the opposite the motive fluid is intended to ADHERE to the disc due electrostatic adherence. Ideally the discs should not be moving faster than the force squared to the proportion of motive fluid pressure from the nozzle. 

It does not make power through RPM as commonly misconstrued by reducing slip you gain torque.

author: @NicolasSicardRoy
text:
You seem to grossly missunderstand how tesla turbines actually work.. 
In a conventional one, the air exits the turbine at the center. The centrifugal force of the air spinning increases the pressure and generate more aerodynamic friction than any modified design would. The rotational force still comes from the direction of the fluid and not from bernouilli's principle. 
The angle you gave to that air dryer just made it turn like it would have made a simple ball turn. 

Tesla turbines are amazing at converting very high pressure into rotational speed and maybe now that we have more efficient gearboxes we could be able to extract some useful torque from it..  

If you want to test your idea to see if the principle work at all, use water.. put it in a river and if it spins up you will know it could work at a bigger scale for air.

author: @Midnight_Dave
text:
I love your rethinking approach!
I never present a problem without at least a conceptual solution, so here is my offering.
When you look at Tesla's actual concept question, it was about efficiency in maximizing power transfer. Thus by injecting a fluid into a cylinder, the near 90 degree angle of attack maximizes the available energy of motion to be captured. And like water draining in a sink, the vortex pattern was his inspiration...
"What if I flattened the vortex funnel into a stack of layers and captured the mechanical energy with the disks?"
That is the fundamental design question and solution.
Based on his design theory/solution, a modification to your design would be a shroud on the face with vanes to induce as close to the optimum 90 degree angle of attack at the edge of the cones. I am thinking like a conch spiral shell (three or four channel?) to minimize back pressure losses.
The whole idea is to recreate the initial confined rotation of the fluid's energy by the external casing of the turbine. And inclusion of a weak venturi collar at the exhaust would assist in preventing back pressure build-up from the mechanically spent working fluid.
Once again, base concept is capture the most energy of a working fluid in rotation as it spirals in it's funnel vortex down a drain.
So I see setting the initial optimal angle of attack as the problem to solve first, and the second being being the reduction of exhaust back-pressure as close to zero or negative if possible. I think you get the idea of the context of the design issues Tesla was trying to address. Angle and flow volume to translate linear differential pressure of a working fluid into rotational torque. Hence flattening the funnel and stacking the design with parallel coupling on a common shaft. Tradeoff is really efficient laminar flow coupling vs centripial force of the working fluid wanting to return to the outer edge. This means for a wind powered system, your design solution is intriging as long as you can get the air moving in the required rotational vector first.

author: @00cho
text: Your Tesla turbine diagram is incorrect. The outflow is through the center hole surrounding the axle. The pipe in your diagram with the arrow pointing out is not an exhaust. It is the reversing input. Both pipes should have a valve, and one or the other is opened, depending on which direction you want the turbine to spin.

author: @perrymattes4285
text: I think you missed an important aspect of the Tesla turbine. Your diagram shows the air exit in the wrong place. The Tesla turbine air exit is axial along the shaft. This causes the air to compress as it spins, increasing the density. This improves the binding to the disks and the efficiency.

author: @n2cat
text: Tesla turbine's discs are of the same size and fluid's pressure and velocity are the same so they simply add up. In the case of concentric tubes system, fluid velocities and pressures vary greatly because of different radii, contact surfaces and angles. The ring layers of air move at different velocities and that results in wide range of force difference in which the smaller ones create a drag on higher due to turbulences.

text: I'm glad to see you're starting to work on this. I'm curious to see how the funnel design will operate but I would personally suggest going the spherical route. Mainly because the flaw Tesla couldn't overcome was that the plates stretching. Vortex shaped plates may have this same issue, but my theory is spherical shaped plates wouldn't because of the strength of the arc. I believe attempting to counteract plate stretch is the reason Schauberger's repulsine plates are shaped the way they are. Thanks as always for the videos!

replies.author: @off6848
replies.text:
Tesla improved on it twice. The problem is people think the power comes from rpm it doesn't power comes by reducing slip which means harmonizing motive fluid pressure and disc rpm regime at peak efficiency. If RPM is your gauge of efficiency then all you're making is a slip machine that will reach speeds that tear it apart or warp it. 

Someone here on youtube managed to produce 6.22ft/lbs of torque at 4500 rpm with his 10 inch setup. Tons of pop scientists on youtube spreading misinformation that you must spin up to 90,000rpm to get anything useful its not true.

author: @Berkeloid0
text: I think this (as-is) would be lower performance than flat discs, because in the flat disc the fluid spirals around the disc, towards the middle, and exits out the centre of the disc.  With the cone design, the fluid can only complete half a turn before it's blown off course by the air coming straight in and exiting straight out again.  So I think you could improve the design by closing it so that once fluid enters the cone, it is free to spiral around multiple times without being blown off course.  This would allow you to extract the most energy from the movement of the fluid, like with the original Tesla design.

replies.author: @danedmiston9673
replies.text: Tesla Turbine type pumps have been on the market for at least 30 years.  (Discflo Pumps)  They have low efficiency (<65%) but do not wear out when pumping abrasive slurry. - good for pumping sand and mud.

replies.author: @na9rz
@danedmiston9673
replies.text: @na9rz The Discflo pumps that I have seen were all industrial size.  The disc in them were spaced as much as 4" apart to let large debris through.  They were fairly low pressure for there size.  I know they have been used for pumping mining waste water, live fish, and product as thick as toothpaste.  Efficiency was never a selling point.

author: @wesbaumguardner8829
text: This is not the same effect as the Tesla turbine.  The Tesla turbine is a stack of flat disks in an enclosed container with the input being on a flat plane with the disk parallel with the tangent of the disks but slightly inset so that it flows into the space between the disks and spirals towards the center of the disk where the outlet is located.  Your turbine has the force being applied practically perpendicular to the surface of the cones which is causing them to rotate.  The Tesla turbine has the force being applied parallel to the surface.  Your turbine is turning due to the impact of the air on the shaped surface of the cones, the Tesla turbine is turning due to the frictional/cohesive forces of the fluid on the disks.  These are two entirely different phenomena.

author: @TheAdeybob
text:
I've a feeling that a rose-petal element might be useful on the outside 'funnel'. It'd help give the whole structure some omni-directional qualities, I should think.
Doesn't have to be too-pronounced an angle....it just has to be enough to provide the work to overcome the inertia, and provide an 'exit' for the flow.
This thing has enough work being directed at it to make it spin, it's just that the energy is essentially hitting a flat surface - but including some kind of subtle petal/wing into the design of the outer disk would act like a small starter motor.
Also, I should imagine that, once spinning fast enough, the device would become effective in an unfeasibly wide cone.
This is some powerful kung fu.

author: @leefoster4133
text: I don't think so mate.  Sorted out the Telsa Turbine and that eight sided tower design in a though experiment weeks ago as part of a unified design weeks ago.  I have even improved the design in the thought experiment.  I still don't have time to muck around with the construction but this you came up with, it's not going to work well.  The fluid has to be perpendicular to what is being used to rotate; right angle.  .5 mm disk x 6 I think, with a separation of .5mm or a bit less.  with a gap on the outside of the disks to housing of something around .1 mm.  On the disk use of impressions in the disk on both sides.  This is to increase the capture of the force for it propel the disk; square pattern, very close, doesn't go very deep; divot is what I call it.  A perfectly flat disk doesn't give it anything to grab on to in order to create the torque.  The gap in between the disks and casing is pressure relief/balance.  Too much pressure on the leading side will cause the disk to warp.

Thats not how it works though read Tesla's patent this machine is not friction based as commonly misconstrued it works on electrostatic adherence its actually an electric turbine because at the electron level it is electrostatically clinging between the boundary layers. A flat as possible disc is the best design. 

You don't actually want friction what you want is to reduce slip and gain torque that means your pressure to disc regime should be optimally harmonized.

author: @mtttony
text: Nice idea! But did you know the Tesla turbine is half of one of Tesla's inventions.The other half of this machine is the Tesla Pump. The turbine and Pump were designed to use what is called Cold Steam. You take a tank of warm water and another tank of similar size. The water tank is connected to the turbine, the turbine to the pump and the pump to the second tank. You purge the second tank of air and the lines,turbine and pump. When you open the valve to purge the air from the water tank. The water starts to boil and creates cold steam. Cold steam is many times the density of air and starts to spin the turbine and in turn the turbine spins the pump. With the pump having more discs then the turbine a low pressure zone is formed behind the turbine. The cause and effect of this is to turn heat directly into kinetic energy with great efficiency. The second tank starts to receive the cold steam but as the temperature has been extracted and turned into kinetic energy. The steam is now colder and therefore the second tank will receive all the cold steam until the majority of the water in the first tank has boiled away.. The turbine and pump is a super efficient heat to kinetic energy converter taking just warm water and most of its thermal energy into movement. You may already know this if you follow the work of Jeremiah ienergysupply. Thanks for your videos and bright ideas TNT..

One big fact I took away from his channel is that steam (and gases in general) actually gets *more* viscous when it gets hotter; huge implications for speed-vs-torque on a tesla turbine.

author: @zfolwick
text: wow!  I suspect that a hyperbolic funnel will increase the acceptable angle of attack of the wind inputs.  In a cone, the only acceptable angle of attack is parallel to the cone angle.  Because the angle of a hyperbolic funnel continuously varies, I think that as long as at SOME point the air input becomes tangential to some part of the funnel, you'll get spinning.  I further suspect that there's a peak torque (when the funnels are already spinning) at an angle that's parallel to the outer edge of the hyperbolic funnel, but with low speeds, and peak speeds (lowest torque) when the wind's coming closest to directly onwards.

You keep getting closer and closer to the design of wind turbine I experimented with heavily: I'm here to mention it once again 😆
It's an 18 blade shrouded propeller (parametric) design found on "thingivers_" I tested 8" diameter (200mm) diameter versions as well as 24" versions (600mm) and was blown away with the efficiency and high/low wind speed performance.  It's worth a print if you'd like a neat looking turbine like propulsion or wind generation propeller design.  I printed it in many scales and in many materials, A PLA 200mm version survived at over 120mph on a testbed mounted to the front bumper of my car, and another on my motorcycle.  People were always asking about it 😆

replies.author: @mrwess1927

replies.author: @Xero1of1
replies.text: @mrwess1927 Not quite. The reason why the cones are able to spin when the wind hits it at an angle is because there is an unequal force applied to the surface of the cone, which makes it spin. The idea of the tesla turbine is to make use of friction across the surface area to make it spin, but if the wind is directly on the cones, you are applying an equal force to the area of the cone which negates any imbalance that would normally cause movement. By adding a couple spiral in between the cones, you're directing a small amount of the wind through that spiral to cause it to start to move, then once it starts moving, the movement of the cone itself should continue the spiral movement of the wind which would then be able to make use of the friction across the surface to promote movement. Movement is caused by imbalance. Newton's third law, right? Except you have to think about in three dimensions...

author: @rayadagio

author: @bmobert
text:
Interesting work. Good idea.
Tesla said that putting washers between the plates made for better acceleration and might make this stack more sensitive to lesser winds.
Other Tesla turbine experimenters claim that for generator-only applications, the circular washers were best replaced with air-foil shaped spacers. Food for thought.
Also, the optimum distance between the surfaces is greatly dependent on the viscosity of the fluid: hot steam I'd different from cold steam, is different from air, is different from water, etc.
Edit-- Let me stress: OPTIMUM distance. IE the distance that is most efficient at coupling with the fluid to extract momentum. A Tesla turbine optimized for air will also work with water, just not optimally so. And, again, it's about viscosity, so scale changes things too.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
that makes me wonder (because I know printing is not going to be perfectly flat something the tesla turbine takes advantage of for higher speed so its surface tension of the air over it, limiting the drag slowing it down) 
***The idea now that I have gone on above LOL)
what about a 3D printed jig or frame that you can then cut say a plastic bottle into sections and form by pushing and bending the flat sheet into, making it form a cone, then the only deformity is going to be that joining point rather than completely covered by ridges from the printing, AND it gives the option too of the form being a continuous sheet that spirals smoothly around with a thin gap between each previous twist, to make that conical shape but the air will now travel around the spiral making contact over and over instead of say 10 cones, so 1/10th of the air touches each cone, the other idea would mean 100% of the air would touch the entire stack of cone like spirals since they are all one single sheet spiraled in an angular fashion to make a completed shape looking similar to your design now. 

I only think by the way the ridges would be an issue if you are actually expecting the lack of drag from roughness to allow super high speeds that tesla turbines use, but using static pressure building up between the plates to force the air to grip onto the plates as it moves around toward it's exit point, then a rougher ribbed surface would actually be beneficial especially if you can either  orient them so it is like a stack of cones touching making up each surface of each cone so you have fractal cone edges making up the surface of each cone, 

and for slower wind input, turn the ridges running from the outer bottom edge of the cone to the inner top edge of your cones in a slight spiral formation so it all forces pushing of the cone on its spindle in one direction. 

I really wish I had a proper lab and workshop set up, and the time to tinker, hopefully soon, though my videos will be over on rumble, as they are already paying out more per video I have up than I have made off any videos on youtube in 10 years. :)

text: QUESTION: Could you position it vertically, so you would  get spin regardless of air direction?

replies.author: @djmccullers
replies.text: @Bob.Jenkins How may old CDs/DVDs do YOU have lying around? I have a bunch! Maybe a good use for them?

replies.author: @MantaRae_Cove
replies.text: Positioned at the ridge of a roof perhaps? Or the peak of a hip-roofed cupola? Could the whole thing be designed into a unit with a weathervane-like tail, similar to old windmills, and allowed to simply self-position? Come to think of it, only an intake nozzle which was positioned slightly out of plane with the turbine would need to align with the wind...

author: @10sheds21
text: Another brilliant idea. You move so fast I'm still way back with Darwin but enjoy these videos so much. Thank you

author: @F.F144
text:
I really really hope you do test to see how much torque it can manage to create!!
Thanks for all the videos!!

replies.author: @UncleKennysPlace
replies.text: Power is the important metric (torque x speed). You can get any specific torque you require through gearing. Power is another thing entirely, and it's what will turn a generator at a usable speed.

replies.author: @F.F144
replies.text: @UncleKennysPlace although what you say is true. If we know how much torque it takes to stop the turbine rotating we can work out how much power it is generating. Is that correct?

replies.author: @unicornadrian1358
replies.text: @F.F144 torque will determine how much load can be pulled before stalling the alternator, yes. Gearing can improve torque but comes at the expense of speed and efficiency due to friction losses in the gearing.
